response.description_long:
Palletmind exists because warehouses rarely buy all their automation from one vendor — automation arrives in waves, and each robot brand brings its own fleet manager, its own map, its own queue. The result is a floor that is locally automated and globally uncoordinated: orders stall while one fleet idles and another backs up. Palletmind sells the coordination layer that makes a mixed fleet behave like one system.

Architecturally it sits in the middle: upward to the WMS — Manhattan and Blue Yonder integrations are productized — and downward to each vendor's fleet manager through its API. The core is a task-allocation solver that re-plans the whole floor every few seconds, balancing travel time, battery state, congestion, and order priority; the nearest robot is not the right robot if it is at 12 percent battery or heading into a reserved intersection. Above the vendors' private maps, a shared traffic map turns intersections, aisle segments, and dock doors into managed resources with reservations, preventing the deadlocks mixed fleets are famous for. Solver decisions are explainable by design — every assignment records the alternatives considered and the costs that decided it.

The digital twin is the adoption wedge. Calibrated from the site's own telemetry and validated by hindcasting last week's floor, it lets operators replay their busiest day against a proposed layout and see throughput before moving a single rack — and it keeps earning after go-live, answering fleet-sizing and peak-season what-ifs by simulation instead of argument.

The claimed results: 20 to 35 percent more picks per hour from the same fleet, and integration in 8 weeks — WMS connection through phased go-live — where a custom middleware build typically takes 6 months. One European 3PL running three robot brands is quoted describing its before-state as three good systems taking turns being the bottleneck. Failure posture is stated plainly: if Palletmind goes down, fleets fall back to their own managers and the floor degrades to its pre-orchestration state, not a standstill.

Customers are 3PLs and retailers operating mixed fleets across North America and Europe, with pricing as an annual license per site, sized by robot count and free of per-task fees.

Palletmind was founded in Atlanta in 2020 by Jonas De Vries and Priya Menon, engineers from an AMR manufacturer who watched their robots idle wherever a competitor's fleet held the work queue. The company is 61 people across Atlanta and Rotterdam, the latter anchoring European deployments where mixed-vendor floors are even more common.
